coredata.dc:title: Superoxide anion turns on the fluorescence of carbon dots-ferric complex for sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Yue, Ji
Peng, Junxiang
Yu, Long
Sun, Mingtai
Sun, Zhenli
Tan, Hua
Wang, Suhua
coredata.dc:description: Superoxide anion (O2 •−) is one of the most important reactive oxygen species and its sensitive detection is of great significance and challenging in the field of environmental and hazardous chemicals monitoring. Herein, we report an off-on fluorescent probe based on ferric functionalized carbon dots (CDs) which were synthesized using p-phenylenediamine as starting material for the detection of O2 •−. The initial green fluorescence of CDs was greatly quenched by Fe3+ based on energy transfer effect on account of the selective formation of ferric complex on the surface of CDs, but not by other metal ions. Interestingly, the fluorescence of CDs could be readily and selectively recovered upon the addition of O2 •−, which can be used for the senstivie detection of superoxide. This type of off-on probe exhibited good photostability and high selectivity toward O2 •− among other interfering substances including metal ions, reactive oxygen species as well as reductant substances. The probe showed good linearity with the concentration of O2 •−, and the limit of detection for O2 •− was estimated down to 25 pM in aqueous solution. Moreover, a paper based sensor for O2 •− was fabricated with a visual detection limit as low as 4.4 nM, demonstrating a promising application for practical detection of superoxide anion.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Superoxide anion
Fluorescent probe
Energy transfer effect
Fluorescence turn-on

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The fabrication of the probe CDs-Fe3+
Preparation of the paper based sensor
Quantitative determination of O2 •− based on turn-on fluorescence
Selectivity of the probe for sensing O2 •−.
Mechanism insights into the off-on probe

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–Vis spectra of CDs and the complex probe, as well as the probe after addition of O2 •−; Green lines represent CDs sample, red lines represent CDs-Fe3+ complexes and the other lines reprensent the CDs-Fe3+–O2 •− complexs formed by continuously adding O2 •−. (b) The photos of color variation from CDs to the complex probe and continuous addition of different concentration of O2 •−.

(a) The fluorescence response of the complex probe towards different concentrations of O2 •− from 0 to 2.7 nM. (b) A linear correlation between the ratio of F/F0 and concentration of O2 •−. The inset photo is taken under a 365 nm UV lamp.

Visual detection of O2 •− via paper sensor. 30 μL of different concentraion of O2 •− are added onto the paper sensor (2 μM, 4 μM, 6 μM, 8 μM, 10 µM, 12 µM). (a) under daylight; (b) the paper indicator loaded with the CDs after addition of Fe3+ without addition of O2 •− as a contrast under 365 nm UV light; (c) photos taken under excitation of 365 nm UV light.

Illustration of CDs-Fe3+ for the detection of O2 •−. The CDs was prepared by hydrothermal synthesis and can be selectively quenched by Fe3+, while the fluorescence of the system CDs-Fe3+ can be turned on upon the addition of O2 •−.

head.ce:title: Superoxide anion turns on the fluorescence of carbon dots-ferric complex for sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Petrochemical Pollution Process and Control, Guangdong University of Petrochemical Technology, Maoming 525000, People’s Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: MOE Key Laboratory of Resources and Environmental System Optimization, College of Environmental Science and Engineering, North China Electric Power University, Beijing 102206, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: MOE Key Laboratory of Resources and Environmental System Optimization
College of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: MOE Key Laboratory of Resources and Environmental System Optimization, College of Environmental Science and Engineering, North China Electric Power University, Beijing 102206, People’s Republic of China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These two authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Superoxide anion (O2 •−) is one of the most important reactive oxygen species and its sensitive detection is of great significance and challenging in the field of environmental and hazardous chemicals monitoring. Herein, we report an off-on fluorescent probe based on ferric functionalized carbon dots (CDs) which were synthesized using p-phenylenediamine as starting material for the detection of O2 •−. The initial green fluorescence of CDs was greatly quenched by Fe3+ based on energy transfer effect on account of the selective formation of ferric complex on the surface of CDs, but not by other metal ions. Interestingly, the fluorescence of CDs could be readily and selectively recovered upon the addition of O2 •−, which can be used for the senstivie detection of superoxide. This type of off-on probe exhibited good photostability and high selectivity toward O2 •− among other interfering substances including metal ions, reactive oxygen species as well as reductant substances. The probe showed good linearity with the concentration of O2 •−, and the limit of detection for O2 •− was estimated down to 25 pM in aqueous solution. Moreover, a paper based sensor for O2 •− was fabricated with a visual detection limit as low as 4.4 nM, demonstrating a promising application for practical detection of superoxide anion.

ce:section.ce:para: Superoxide anion (O2 •−), a by-product in the generation of ATP of the microenvironment in the human body, plays a vital role in mediating biochemistry and organic pathology [1]. However, exposure to excessive O2 •− would oxidize biological membranes, tissue and organism and cause diseases such as cancer, diabetes and hepatitis [2-4]. Besides, it shows special property that function as precursor and stimulate conversion between other reactive oxygen species (ROS) such as hydroxyl radical, peroxide (H2O2) and so on encourage the accurate and quick detection [5-13]. In the past few decades, various approaches have been developed to detect O2 •−, such as electron spin resonance, spectrophotometry, chemiluminescence and chromatography [14-16]. It is worth mentioning that enzyme-based electrochemical biosensors take advantage of specific enzyme for O2 •− such as superoxide dismutase and has been used among various research fields [2,17-19]. Moreover, the expensive instrumental based approaches are inconvenient and not practical for on-site detection of O2 •− [20]. Therefore, it is still in huge demand to develop a simple, rapid, and on-site detection method for O2 •− both in environments and biological research. Compared with these methods, fluorescence-based sensors exhibit more advantages such as high selectivity and sensitivity as well as visual detection [21], which are more favorable for the detection of O2 •−. Several fluorescent probes have been developed and most of which were based on specific organic molecules such as hydroethidine and naphthofluorescein to achieve high sensitivity and selectivity [22,23].

Carbon dots have recently attracted increasing attention owing to their property of easy preparation, cost-effective, and excellent photostabilty [24-26]. Unlike other fluorescent nanomaterials, the synthesis of carbon dots does not need to consider of elemental scarcity, complicated, tedious, cost-sensitive and even invalid preparation steps [27]. Furthermore, it can be easily engineered as chemosensors through grafting on the surface, a layer of reactive site including organic molecules and other nanometarials that are responsive toward analytes [28-30]. Our group has developed several fluorescent probes based on CDs for selective and quantitative detection of bioactive molecules, through molecular engineering of inorganic CDs with different functional group [31,32]. We found that CDs can act as a good reference fluorophore whose fluorescence cannot be quenched by many interfering substances. Gao et al developed a ratiometric fluorescent biosensor for O2 •−, using hydroethidine as recognition and response site and CDs as reference fluorophore [22]. Tellurium doped CDs was synthesized by Zhang et al to instantly detect O2 •− with high selectivity and sensibility [33]. In fact, few carbon dots based sensors have been reported for the detection of O2 •−, and it is on demand especially for the field of environmental and physiological research.

In this work, we report an unprecedented example of CDs based fluorescent nanoprobe for selective and quantitative detection of O2 •−. The probe was facilely fabricated as a combination of ferric and carbon dots with very weak fluorescence. Upon the addition of O2 •−, the fluorescence could be restored effectively, resulting in selective fluorescence enhancement at 556 nm. This turn-on probe has demonstrated to be highly sensitive and selective to O2 •− detection. Furthermore, a paper-based sensor has been developed for the visual detection of O2 •−. The fabrication of the probe and its mechanism for the sensing of O2 •− are illustrated in scheme 1 .

P-phenylenediamine (p-PDA) was purchased from Sigma. Ethyl acetate, petroleum ether, tert-Butyl hydroperoxide (TBHP), sodium hypochlorite (NaClO), potassium dioxide (KO2) vitamin C (Vc), sodium sulfite (Na2SO3), and glutathione (GSH) were obtained from Aladdin Reagent Co. Ltd. Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), acetonitrile (ACN), ethanol (EtOH) and ferrous sulfate heptahydrate (FeSO4·7H2O), ferric chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O), calcium chloride dihydrate (CaCl2·2H2O), hexahydrate (MgCl2·6H2O), manganese chloride (MnCl2), sodium chloride (NaCl), potassium chloride (KCl), zinc chloride (ZnCl2) and copper chloride dehydrate (CuCl2·2H2O) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. All chemicals were used as received without further purification unless otherwise specified. Ultrapure water (18.25 MΩ•cm) was produced with a Millipore water purification system. Phosphate buffer with different pH were prepared via dihydrogen phosphate dehydrate (NaH2PO4) and hydrogen phosphate dodecahydrate (Na2HPO4). Fluorescence spectra and UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded at room temperature by a Perkin-Elmer LS-55 luminescence spectrometer and Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrometer, respectively. The structures and morphologies of CDs were examined using a JEOL 2010 transmission electron microscope. Infrared (IR) spectra were performed on a Thermo Fisher Nicolet iS10 FT-IR spectrometer. XPS measurements were carried out with a Thermo ESCALAB 250 high performance electron spectrometer with an Al Kα (1486.6 eV) radiation. Digital photos were taken by a Canon 350 D digital camera.

CDs was synthesized according to solvothermal method with some modification [37]. Briefly, 0.4 g of p-PDA was dissolved in 40 ml EtOH to form a homogeneous solution, and the resultant light pink transparent solution was immediately transferred into a poly (tetrafluoroethylene)-lined autoclave. After heated at 200 °C for 12 h and then cooled down to room temperature naturally, the mixture was purified through a silica chromatography column using ethyl acetate as eluent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The fabrication of the probe CDs-Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: Owing to the fact that the performance of the CDs would be inflected by the environment, such as solvent and pH, thus the optimal media and parameters for the detection of O2 •− has been tested and determined. First, the CDs was dissolved in aqueous solution emitting red fluorescence, O2 •− could selectively recover the fluorescence quenched by Fe3+ (Fig. S1). Subsequently, the CDs were prepared in phosphate buffer solution (PB) with different pH to observe fluorescence response to O2 •−. Take the solvothermal method of carbon dots into consideration, a series of solvents was employed to optimize the experiment conditions for high sensitivity of O2 •−. Owing to solvent-dependent phenomenon, carbon nanodots emit green fluorescence dissolved in organic solvents such as EtOH, DMF, DMSO, and ACN. However, only in the solvent of acetonitrile, trace concentration of O2 •− could turn on the fluorescence of carbon dots. Thus, all the subsequent experiments were conducted in the solution of acetonitrile. The probe CDs-Fe3+ was prepared via addition of 3 µL of Fe3+ (2 mM) into CDs solution. The final concentration of Fe3+ was 3 µM.

The O2 •− was obtained by directly dissolving KO2 in DMSO [22]. For the selectivity test, hypochlorite ion (ClO−) was produced by NaClO, singlet oxygen (1O2) was produced by H2O2 and NaClO. The concentration of H2O2 and NaClO were also measured through analysis of UV spectra. Hydroxyl radical (•OH) was generated by Fenton reaction via adding Fe2+ in the presence of H2O2. Peroxynitrite (ONOO−) was prepared according to previously reported literatures [34,35]. Tert-butyl peroxyl radical (TBO•) was freshly prepared by addition of Fe2+ in the presence of TBHP [36,37]. Various metal ion solutions were prepared in ultrapure water. O2 •− as well as other interfering substances were directly added or produced in the probe solution respectively, and fluorescence spectra were recorded from 450 nm to 680 nm with slit width of 10 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the paper based sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The paper based sensor was obtained from polyvinylidene fluoride microporous filter membrane. Several pieces of round paper membrane with a diameter of 6 mm were prepared beforehand for later use. The round test paper was loaded with the solution of probe CDs-Fe3+ and was further treated with filter paper to be desiccative, followed by keeping in dark for future use. The continuous changes in brightness were observed and recorded with 365 nm UV light.

The synthesis of CDs was carried out using p-PDA as starting material based on solvothermal method. TEM and FT-IR analysis were performed to characterize the structure of the green-emitting CDs. The TEM image and the size distribution map showed that the CDs have good monodispersity without aggregation and possess approximately 2–3 nm in size (Fig. S2). From the FT-IR spectra as shown in Fig. S3(a), we can see that the peaks around 3380–3180 cm−1 indicate the existence of N–H or O–H group on the surface of the CDs, which provides abundant reactive sites for chelation with various metal ions [38]. In addition, C–H (2916 cm−1), C = N (1622 cm−1), benzene ring skeleton (1514 cm−1), aromatic ether (1270 cm−1) and C–O (1106 cm−1) are observed for the CDs. XPS analysis was also carried on to further study the chemical composition of CDs. Fig. S3(b) illustrated the three main constituent elements of the CDs, namely C, H and O. As shown in Fig. S3(c), the deconvolution of C 1 s band presents four peaks at 284.7, 285.4, 286.1, and 288.0 eV, corresponding to sp2 carbons (C = C), sp3 carbons (C-N, C-O), and carbonyl carbons (C = O), respectively. The N 1 s centred at 398.6, 399.2, and 400.2 eV, were assigned to pyridyl N, amino N, and pyrrolyl N, respectively (Fig. S3(d)) [39].

By evaluating the performance of the prepared carbon dots, we found that Fe3+ could effectively quench the fluorescence of CDs and form a novel complex with weak fluorescence. However, the complex could be selectively turned on with the addition of O2 •− in PB solution as well as other organic solvents. Particularly, a significant fluorescence enhancement was observed with 2.7 nM O2 •−. The UV–Vis absorption spectra of CDs without and with the addition of Fe3+ as well as the mixture system CDs-Fe3+–O2 •− were investigated, as shown in Fig. 1 (a). The prepared carbon dots dissolved in ACN display the absorption maxima at 280 nm similar to the previously reported fluorescent carbon dots, which could be attributed to π-π* transition of aromatic double bond [29,40,41]. Besides, there is a typical absorption band at 473 nm. It can be seen that the addition of Fe3+ to CDs solution resulted in one new broad absorption band at 571 nm, accompanied by decreasing of the original peak at 473 nm. In addition, the absorption band at 280 nm belongs to aromatic double bond red-shifts to 300 nm, suggesting noteworthy variation in electronic properties or spatial structures of the surface group around the CDs. These results could be attributed to the formation of complexes between ferric ions and the surface groups around the CDs. While the three absorption bands decreased with the addition of O2 •−, resulting in almost the identical absorption spectrum of the CDs. Fig. 1(b) shows the color change of the CDs solution, the CDs-Fe3+ complexes solution and the CDs-Fe3+–O2 •− complexes solution with the addition of O2 •− under daylight, respectively.

Considering that the CDs have emission maxima at 556 nm in ACN, which is adequately overlapped with the absorption spectrum of CDs-Fe3+ mixture, whose absorbance greatly decreased after reaction with O2 •−. Such a characteristic is favorable for constructing a fluorescence turn-on system for O2 •− based on the energy transfer effect between CDs and the complexes formed on its surface group. Most importantly, the fluorescence and absorption of CDs was not affected by O2 •− even at the high concentration (Fig. S4). Based on these facts, a hybrid probe CDs-Fe3+ was fabricated facilely by adding Fe3+ to the solution of the CDs to form stable complexes. The probe showed very weak fluorescence with emission maxima at 556 nm in acetonitrile, suggesting successful construction of the energy transfer system. The low background is favorable for the application of fluorescence detection on the basis of flurorescence turn-on mechanism. As shown in Fig. S5(a), the lines on the left and right represent the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of CDs in three conditions, respectively. The emission spectra display that the emission peak of CDs locates at 547 nm, and the fluorescence is quenched by adding an appropriate amount of Fe3+ to form the complex probe CDs-Fe3+. Upon the addition of O2 •−, the fluorescence intensity of the complex probe increased by nearly 17 fold, showing excellent fluorescent enhancement effect. In addition, the prepared probe has good photostability in this sensing system (Fig. S5(b)) and under different pH conditions (Fig. S6). The fluorescence intensity of the probe remained constant upon irradiation with excitation lights every 5 min for 1 h, and even once a day for one month for CDs when stored in refrigerator (Fig. S7). These properties made the as-prepared hybrid complex a good fluorescent probe for O2 •− detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative determination of O2 •− based on turn-on fluorescence
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence behavior of the probe CDs-Fe3+ with the addition of O2 •− were carefully studied. All of the fluorescence spectra were recorded under excitation wavelength at 365 nm and emission wavelength at 556 nm. The response time of the probe toward O2 •− were first investigated prior to the sensitivity study (Fig. S8). The result shows the probe could be turned on immediately upon the addition of O2 •−. As shown in Fig. 2 , the fluorescence intensity of the probe exhibits good linearity with the concentration of O2 •− in the range of 0.3–2.4 nM with a coefficient of determination of 0.9985, which can be used for the quantification of O2 •−. Quantitative analysis of the method showed a limit of detection for O2 •− at 25 pM, which was estimated on the basis of the definition of the three times of the blank signal deviation (3σ). These results proved that the complex probe could detect O2 •− with high sensitivity. In addition, this fluorescence enhancement could be easily visualized with the probe solution changing from non-fluorescence color to yellow green under a 365 nm UV lamp. In addition, the comparison of the linear range and detection limit of several metal doped carbon dots employed for O2 •− sensing [33,42] has been exhibited in Tables S1. It can be seen that the linear range of CDs- Fe3+ is wider than that of Te-CDs while narrower than other probes. And CDs- Fe3+ exhibits lower detection limit than other probes. In general, CDs- Fe3+ possesses good analytical capabilities for O2 •− sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the probe for sensing O2 •−.
ce:section.ce:para: To study and confirm the specificity of the detection system for O2 •−, the responses of the probe toward various metal ions as well as reactive oxygen species were investigated, as shown in Fig. S9. Apparently, there are no changes after the addition of Ca2+ (CaCl2), Cu2+ (CuCl2), Fe3+ (FeCl3), K+ (KCl), Mg2+ (MgCl2), Zn2+ (ZnCl2), Mn2+ (MnCl2), Na+ (NaCl), H2O2, ClO− (NaClO), •OH, ONOO−, 1O2, TBO•, TBHP, Na2SO3, vitamin C, and GSH, respectively. Fe3+ were set as one of the interference factors to verify that the prepared probe functionalized with Fe3+ would not be affected by Fe3+. On the contrary, the fluorescence was dramatically turned-on upon the addition of 2.7 nM O2 •− (Fig. S10). In addition, in order to further confirm the validity of the method, anti-interference study of the probe to O2 •− was performed by adding 10 fold mixture of ROS and RNS as well as metal ions, respectively. The results demonstrated that the probe has good selectivity toward O2 •− and excessive interference has no obvious influence on the sensing behavior of the probe. Herein, this proposed probe is expected to allow potential application for the detection of O2 •−.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism insights into the off-on probe
ce:section.ce:para: To examine the sensing mechanism, quenching performance on carbon dots with the addition of various metal ions were investigated. It was found that only Fe3+ can efficiently quench the fluorescence of carbon dots (Fig. S11). It is reasonable that electron-deficient ferric ion would form stable complexes with the surface groups of the electron-rich CDs [43]. Energy transfer effect is proposed to explain for the quenching mechanism. The compactness of the carbon dots structure is weaken due to the formation of CDs-Fe3+ complexes, which shut off the pathway of energy transfer and finally lead to fluorescence quenching [44]. After addition of O2 •−, CDs-Fe3+ complexes were reduced to CDs-Fe2+ whose absorption band did not overlap with the emission of the CDs (Fig. S12) [45], which result in fluorescence turn on. Compared with the complex probe CDs-Fe3+, the FT-IR spectrum of the probe after addition of O2 •− showed significant enhancement of stretching vibration of C-N/C-O (1000 cm−1-1300 cm−1) (Fig. S13). In addition, the O–H vibration showed broader peak for the probe after addition of O2 •− than the probe itself. XPS test of Fe before and after addition of O2 •− was carried out. Both of high resolution of Fe 2p3/2 analysis can be fitted into two peaks at around 711.8 eV for Fe3+ and 715.0 eV for surface ferric complexes (Fig. S14) [46,47]. It is worth noting that the peak intensity of Fe3+ after addition of O2 •− is weaker than that of the probe before reaction with O2 •−. These results demonstrated that Fe3+ has been consumed with O2 •−, which support our proposed mechanism hypothesis. However, the content of the reduction product CDs-Fe2+ is less due to the low content of O2 •− added to the probe. Thus, the peak for Fe2+ is not easy to observe from XPS test.

In order to further study the role of Fe(III) in the sensing system, a comparison of fluorescence responses of the probe to O2 •− as well as CDs upon addition of Fe2+ was carefully examined, as shown in Fig. S15. The fluorescence of CDs was quenched after addition of Fe3+ with the emission maxima shifts from 547 nm to 556 nm. While the fluorescence was turned on but the emission maxima did not return to the initial wavelength upon addition of O2 •−. We speculated that the reduction product of CDs-Fe3+ complexes by addition of O2 •− would exist in the form of CDs-Fe2+ according to previous report [45]. In order to verify this speculation, the fluorescence response of CDs with the addition of Fe2+ was studied and observed. The intensity was almost unchanged, but the emission maxima shift from 547 nm to 556 nm which was in consistent with that of CDs-Fe3+ complex upon addition of O2 •−. The results support the hypothesis of specific reduction of CDs-Fe3+ by O2 •− to a certain extent.

O2 •− is a primary oxidant that would cause combustion and explosion in the presence of flammable, organics, reductants and even water vapor. Thus, to develop and fabricate a sensor for on-site and instant detection of O2 •− is of great significance. On this account, a paper-based sensor has been developed to realize the aim of quick visual detection for O2 •−. We used a punching device to prepare the round paper with the same size as O2 •− indicator, and different concentrations of O2 •− (2 μM, 4 μM, 6 μM, 8 μM, 10 µM, 12 µM) were prepared in advance. The round paper was thoroughly immersed in the probe solution for 30 s to load the complex probe on the paper. Then 30 μL of different concentration of O2 •− solutions were transferred to the surface of the paper sensor and subsequently dealt to be desiccative by filter paper. As shown in Fig. 3 , continuous variation of brightness could be observed by naked eyes from the paper sensor under a 365 nm UV illumination. Fig. 3(a) displayed the paper sensor under daylight, Fig. 3(b) showed the paper sensor after immersing in the CDs-Fe3+ complexes probe solution, and Fig. 3(c) showed the paper sensor after the addition of different concentrations of O2 •−. The detection limit of the paper indicator was calculated to be 4.4 nM by the definition of the least concentration of O2 •− stacked on the surface of CDs-Fe3+ spot that lead to a distinctive change of fluorescence intensity.

In conclusion, a fluorescent probe based on ferric ion functionalized CDs was designed and prepared for the sensing of O2 •−. The N–H and O–H group on the surface of CDs chelated with Fe3+ to form ferric complexes which would quench the initial fluorescence of CDs based on energy transfer mechanism. In the presence of O2 •−, the ferric complex was specifically reduced and the energy transfer process was thus shut off which turned on the fluorescence of the probe. This probe has been demonstrated with excellent sensitivity and selectivity to O2 •− detection. A good detection limit as low as 25 pM was obtained. This fluorescence turn-on probe shows several advantages including sensitivity, selectivity, photostability over other reported fluorecent probe. Besides, this probe has been fabricated on an indicatior paper for the visual detection of O2 •−, which shows great potential in practical application.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge the financial support from the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2017YFA0207003), and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21775042 and 21475134).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2021.106412.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
